content:
I've been thinking about how to write linked list bindings. The current ones need updating because there are some remaining soundness issues and it doesn't use pin-init. Here are my thoughts:

The links field of a struct has these components:

  1. Prev/next pointers.
  2. Some way to synchronize concurrent insertions.
  3. Some way to identify the type of element and perform a container_of.

In Binder, I have these use-cases:

  1. Boxed values that can be pushed to just one linked list. We need mutable access.
  2. Reference counted values that are pushed to a linked list where the concrete type is known. The type also needs to be enqueued to a workqueue at the same time.
  3. A trait implemented by many types where the linked list can hold any implementer of the type.
  4. A list like the one in use-case 3, except that it holds only one specific concrete type. Ideally, we want to use the same link pointers as use-case 3.

Based on the above use-cases and inspired by the workqueue, I designed this API that supports all of them:

struct LinkedList<T: LLItemPtr<ID>, const ID: u64>;

#[repr(C)]
struct LinkPtrs {
    next: *mut LinkPtrs,
    prev: *mut LinkPtrs,
}

trait LLItemPtr<const ID: u64> {
    type PushResult;
    // If the first argument is none, then inserting failed. In either case,
    // pushing returns the push result.
    fn try_into_raw(self) -> (Option<NonNull<LinkPtrs>>, Self::PushResult);
    fn from_raw(ptr: *mut LinkPtrs) -> Self;

    type InnerRef<'a>;
    fn access_raw<'a>(ptr: *mut LinkPtrs) -> Self::InnerRef<'a>;

    // Mutable cursors always make sense. Even if we can't mutate the item, we
    // can still remove it from the list.
    type CursorMutRef<'a>;
    fn access_with_cursor<'a>(ptr: *mut LinkPtrs) -> Self::CursorMutRef<'a>;
}

/// Implemented for types where a mutable iterator makes sense.
trait LLIterMutRef<const ID: u64>: LLItemPtr<ID> {
    type IterMutRef<'a>;
    fn access_with_iter<'a>(ptr: *mut LinkPtrs) -> Self::IterMutRef<'a>;
}

For the first use-case, we can have this helper:

struct LLBox<T> {
    inner: Box<LLBoxInner<T>>,
}

struct LLBoxInner<T> {
     links: LinkPtrs,
     value: T,
}

impl<const ID: u64, T> LLItemPtr<ID> for LLBox<T> {
    type PushResult = ();
    type InnerRef<'a> = &'a T;
    type CursorMutRef<'a> = &'a mut T;
    type IterMutRef<'a> = &'a mut T;
}

impl<const ID: u64, T> LLItemPtr<ID> for Pin<LLBox<T>> {
    type PushResult = ();
    type InnerRef<'a> = Pin<&'a T>;
    type CursorMutRef<'a> = Pin<&'a mut T>;
    type IterMutRef<'a> = Pin<&'a mut T>;
}

For the reference counted use-cases, we can have the following API:

trait LLItem<Ptr, const ID: u64> {
    type ContainerOfStrategy: ContainerOfStrategy<Self, ID>;
}

struct Links<S, D, const ID: u64> {
    links: LinkPtrs,
    /// Used to synchronize insertions to several lists in parallel.
    sync: S,
    /// Extra data used for the container_of operation.
    data: D,
}

trait HasLinks<const ID: u64> {
    const OFFSET: usize;
    type Data;
    type Sync;
}

// Or use named wrappers around AtomicBool and ().
type ArcLinks<D = (), const ID: u64 = 0> = Links<AtomicBool, D, ID>;
type BoxLinks<D = (), const ID: u64 = 0> = Links<(), D, ID>;

impl<const ID: u64, T: ?Sized> LLItemPtr<ID> for Arc<T>
where
    T: LLItem<Arc<T>, ID>,
    T: HasLinks<Sync = AtomicBool, ID>,
{
    type PushResult = Result<(), Arc<T>>;
    type InnerRef<'a> = ArcBorrow<'a, T>;
    type CursorMutRef<'a> = ArcBorrow<'a, T>;
}

// Giving out mutable access to the contents while it is in the list is
// problematic. You could overwrite the links pointers.
//
// Even if we give the links pointers a destructor, its still problematic.
// Sure, we can remove the item from the list, but this leaks the allocation.
impl<const ID: u64, T: ?Sized> LLItemPtr<ID> for Box<T>
where
    T: LLItem<Box<T>, ID>,
    T: HasLinks<ID>,
{
    type PushResult = ();
    type InnerRef<'a> = &'a T;
    type CursorMutRef<'a> = &'a T;
}

impl<const ID: u64, T: ?Sized> LLItemPtr<ID> for Pin<Box<T>>
where
    T: LLItem<Pin<Box<T>>, ID>,
    T: HasLinks<ID>,
{
    type PushResult = ();
    type InnerRef<'a> = Pin<&'a T>;
    type CursorMutRef<'a> = Pin<&'a T>;
}

trait ContainerOfStrategy<T, const ID: u64>{
    fn container_of(ptr: *mut LinkPtrs) -> *mut T;
}

enum SizedTypeCOS {}
impl<T, const: ID: u64> ContainerOfStrategy<T, ID> for SizedTypeCOS
where
    T: HasLinks<ID> + Sized,
{ ... }

enum MetadataCOS {}
impl<T, const: ID: u64> ContainerOfStrategy<T, ID> for MetadataCOS
where
    T: HasLinks<ID, Data = <T as Pointee>::Metadata> + ?Sized,
{ ... }

// or without ptr_metadata feature
enum SelfPtrCOS {}
impl<T, const: ID: u64> ContainerOfStrategy<T, ID> for SelfPtrCOS
where
    T: HasLinks<ID, Data = NonNull<T>> + ?Sized,
{ ... }

This solve each use-case:

Usecase 1. Using LLBox lets us do this. (We can't use a normal Box because it doesn't give mutable access.)

Usecase 2. We can do this like this:

struct MyStruct {
    #[pin]
    links: ArcLinks,
}

impl_has_links! {
    impl HasLinks for MyStruct {
        type Sync = AtomicBool;
        self.links
    }
}

impl LLItem<Arc<Self>> for MyStruct {
    type ContainerOfStrategy = SizedTypeCOS;
}

Usecase 3. We can do this like this:

struct Transaction {
    #[pin]
    links: Links<AtomicBool, Meta>,
}

impl_has_links! {
    impl HasLinks for Transaction {
        type Sync = AtomicBool;
        type Data = Meta;
        self.links
    }
}

type Meta = <dyn MyTrait as Pointee>::Metadata;
// or we could use NonNull<Transaction> as the extra data.

trait MyTrait: HasLinks<Sync = AtomicBool, Data = Meta> { ... }

impl LLItem<Arc<dyn MyTrait>> for dyn MyTrait {
    type ContainerOfStrategy = MetadataCOS;
}

Usecase 4.

We also add this impl:

impl LLItem<Arc<Self>> for Transaction {
    // This doesn't care about the type of the extra data, so it's okay that it
    // is `Meta`.
    type ContainerOfStrategy = SizedTypeCOS;
}

Simpler alternatives:

We can also move further towards the existing linked list solution. With that solution, we always perform the atomic CAS when inserting into the list even when the items are unique. It also doesn't support use-case 4.

Another alternative is to provide two completely separate linked list implementations. One of them only works with LLBox and the other only works with Arc. There are some advantages to this, e.g. we could let the user insert a UniqueArc into a linked list of Arcs to avoid the CAS on insert. The only disadvantages are code duplication and that we can't easily support new smart pointers. (Note that this is similar to the rbtree, where we currently only have what is analogous to the LLBox-only implementation.)

Thoughts?
First thought is spell out names of the types :) I’ll take a closer look next week.
Not following the mutable cursor ref here. Can you explain that further?
Basically, the mutable cursor will have a method that removes an item from the linked list. This is always possible, even if the item is an Arc and hence immutable.
So would it insert the item again when the cursor moves to the next item? I assume a cursor would be something that moves across the list ?
Could you provide usage examples?
Also, maybe move the proposal to github or hackmd?

Andreas Hindborg said:

So would it insert the item again when the cursor moves to the next item? I assume a cursor would be something that moves across the list ?

No, it would be like calling pop in the middle of the list. The item is removed for good, and the cursor moves to the next or prevoius item.

For example, if you have a list of Arc<MyStruct> with each struct having an id, then you can remove the item with a given id by using a mutable cursor to move through the list to find the item with the right id, then remove it from the list.
There's no race because it requires mutable access to the list itself, so there can only be one such cursor at any given time. (The struct could be accessed in parallel, but the links field cannot.)
@Alice Ryhl for the first case, do you need the list to be doubly linked? or would it be sufficient for the cursor to be able to remove the current item without backtracking?
I have assumed that all lists are doubly linked, and haven't really considered if I can get away with single links.

I think if you do not want to walk the list backwards and only want to scan through the list, searching for a particular item that you want to remove, then a singly linked list would be much easier for the first case (and an argument to split the design)
I also sometimes remove items from the middle of the list, which requires walking the list if singly linked.
Like, given the Arc<MyStruct> and LinkedList, I call remove
I am just talking about the first case with Box
Right, there are definitely some cases where a singly linked list is enough.
So it's true that we could split the design in that way too.
Since we dont need unsafe code for such a list, I think we could just split that off

Writing it safely is probably pretty annoying because you need a pointer to the current and previous element when searching for something to remove, but I agree. And even if that is done unsafely, it's still a minor amount of unsafe.
You do not, you can simply just have a pointer to the previous element and then do a double deref although that might not perfom as well...
anyways thats implementation details
is the cursor concept the same as described here? https://rust-unofficial.github.io/too-many-lists/sixth-cursors-intro.html

the concept should be similar, but I havent read that document.

a cursor is just an iterator that "remembers" its position and allows to perform list operation at that position.

Currently I'm just using the old linked list bindings from the rust branch. However, my flamegraphs are showing that modifying the list is crazy slow compared to what C is doing. Any thoughts on how we should design our linked list bindings so that it optimizes well?
IIUC you are using raw_list.rs, right? Because there we have an atomic bool that is used to ensure that the entry is inserted only in a single list
Yes.
how does the C side ensure that? is it just through convention and praying that it works?
I think they just hold the process lock when touching the list.
I see, do you have a link to your usage of the bindings?

For example:
https://github.com/Darksonn/linux/blob/ddaabdf0951cb88d940dff61ef7d5f83b6122cc2/drivers/android/thread.rs#L297

or this one:
https://github.com/Darksonn/linux/blob/ddaabdf0951cb88d940dff61ef7d5f83b6122cc2/drivers/android/range_alloc.rs#L142

Alice Ryhl said:

I think they just hold the process lock when touching the list.

Hmm.. haven't checked the code yet (it was a while ago), but I think the list locks don't protect the attempt of inserting one entry into two lists?

I have taken a look and found several things:

  1. our current list implementation does not conform to miri, it gets the pointer provenance wrong and errors when inserting just two elements.
  2. for the range_alloc usage, I think it would be sufficient to have a more rusty version without intrusion, what do you think? Do we want two different lists in the kernel?
  3. for the other cases, I would have to take a closer look, maybe something similar to LockedBy can be reused there.
regarding 2, I have implemented a rusty list, if you are interested, I can push it.
Please do ;-) I think it makes sense to have a simple linked list if we don't need the interoperatiblity on the existing C code.
@Alice Ryhl do you know if the codepath of adding an item to a list that already is in one gets triggered by your use case?
Yes, there is one case where it can happen.
because if it does not, you could maybe try and see what happens if you remove the atomic bool
I already tried replacing it with an UnsafeCell<bool>, which improves performance somewhat.

Alice Ryhl said:

Yes, there is one case where it can happen.

oh I see, because aside from the atomic bool, I am not so sure what the perf issue source could be

Alice Ryhl said:

Yes, there is one case where it can happen.

where exactly could it happen?

Alice Ryhl said:

I already tried replacing it with an UnsafeCell<bool>, which improves performance somewhat.

that is interesting. as I said before, I think I have a solution for range_alloc that does not require any synchronization, since it only supports adding Boxed values, so if that is the main performance bottle neck then I could polish that and push it
The main performance bottleneck is the Arc<dyn DeliverToRead> stuff. Another perf issues was the get_links call, whose fix had an even bigger perf improvement.
Besides, the range allocator should probably be a rbtree and not a linked list at all.

Alice Ryhl said:

The main performance bottleneck is the Arc<dyn DeliverToRead> stuff. Another perf issues was the get_links call, whose fix had an even bigger perf improvement.

I see, then I will take a look at that.

Just a status update: I got around to splitting up the linked list into several patches. I was a bit surprised about how many patches I ended up with. Check it out at https://github.com/Darksonn/linux/commits/b4/linked-list/
